candidate_problems.text: What is the non-circular criterion for saying that an analogy has produced a genuinely new abstraction layer rather than merely redescribed old content at a different level of language? The conjecture makes novelty depend on emergence of a higher-order schema and transformed representational repertoire, but it leaves open how to distinguish real knowledge growth from post hoc verbal unification. This is the central unresolved tension because the whole conjecture turns on whether 'new self-referential modeling capacity' can be identified independently of the reasoner's own impression of insight.

Is the strange-loop/self-referential step actually necessary for creative analogical discovery, or only one important mechanism among several? The conjecture treats bidirectional redescription and recursive reorganization of the reasoner's representational space as load-bearing, but many apparently fruitful analogies seem productive without obvious self-modeling or retroactive reinterpretation of the source. Exploring whether the loop is necessary, sufficient, or just a common accompaniment would test the conjecture's strongest claim.

What ontology of abstract structure is required for analogies to 'discover' real invariants across domains, and how can that claim be reconciled with the possibility that schemas are constructed by the reasoner rather than found? The conjecture relies on the reality of higher-order patterns to explain why analogy yields knowledge instead of projection, but it does not resolve the tension between realism about abstract structure and the mind-dependent reorganization of representational repertoire. This matters because without a clearer account, the conjecture risks oscillating between discovery and invention.
